question: How many missions are there in The Crew?
gold_answer: 50 missions, found in the logbook. They get progressively harder and are meant to be played in order, but can be played out of order.

question: What card suits are in the game?
gold_answer: Five suits: pink, blue, green, yellow (color cards, values 1-9), and rocket cards (values 1-4).

question: If I can win a trick, am I forced to?
gold_answer: No. You are never required to win a trick — if you have multiple playable cards you may play a low card even when a higher card would have won.

question: How do rocket cards work in a trick?
gold_answer: Rockets are trump cards and win any trick. In a color-suited trick a rocket may only be played if you cannot follow the color suit. If a rocket leads, rockets must be followed if possible. If several rockets are played, the highest-value rocket wins.

question: Can we look back at tricks we've already won?
gold_answer: Won tricks are set aside face down; you may only look at the cards from the most recently won trick.

question: How many times can each player communicate per mission?
gold_answer: Each crew member may communicate exactly once per mission attempt (one radio communication token each). If the attempt fails, the token can be used again in the next attempt.

question: When exactly is communication allowed?
gold_answer: Only after the task distribution phase, and then any time before a trick begins — never during a trick.

question: What does the position of the radio communication token on a communicated card mean?
gold_answer: Top of the card: it is your highest card of that color. Middle: your only card of that color. Bottom: your lowest card of that color. One of these must apply or the card may not be communicated.

question: Can I communicate a rocket card?
gold_answer: No. Rocket cards may never be communicated.

question: My communicated 'highest' card later became my only card of that color. Do I move the token?
gold_answer: No. Radio communication token placement may never be changed, even if the statement is no longer accurate.

question: What are the reminder cards for?
gold_answer: After communicating, you take a reminder card into your hand to remind you that your communicated card is still on the table. When you play the communicated card, you put the reminder card down and flip your radio token to the red side. Reminder cards look different on both sides so others can see you hold one.

question: How are cards dealt with three players?
gold_answer: The 40 playing cards are dealt evenly; with three players one person gets one extra card. After the last trick, the one leftover card remains unplayed.

question: Who is the commander?
gold_answer: Whoever holds the rocket 4 announces it and is commander for the mission, taking the commander token. The commander chooses a task card first and starts the first trick.

question: How are task cards distributed?
gold_answer: The specified number of task cards are placed face up in the center. The commander picks first, then each crew member takes one in turn until all are distributed. A player can end up with several tasks, and players won't necessarily have equal numbers.

question: When is a task fulfilled?
gold_answer: When the crew member who owns the task card wins a trick containing the matching playing card. The task card is then turned face down. Several tasks can be fulfilled in the same trick.

question: What happens if the wrong player wins a card that matches someone else's task?
gold_answer: You lose immediately and must restart the mission from the beginning: reshuffle all cards, deal new hands, and take new tasks.

question: Who starts each trick?
gold_answer: The commander starts the first trick. Every subsequent trick is started by whoever won the previous trick.

question: How does the distress signal work?
gold_answer: At the start of a mission — after dealing playing cards and task cards but before anyone communicates — the crew may jointly decide to send a distress signal. Flip the token to its active (satellite) side and circle the distress signal field in the logbook. Each crew member passes one card to a neighbor (all in the same, jointly chosen direction); rockets may not be passed. It costs +1 on the recorded number of attempts.

question: Does the distress signal stay active if we fail the attempt?
gold_answer: Yes. Once activated it remains active until the current mission is completed — at the start of each new attempt cards may again be passed. It is only flipped back to inactive when a new mission begins.

question: What happens if we violate a task token's condition?
gold_answer: Violating a task token condition (e.g. task order) means the mission immediately fails — you have lost that attempt.

question: Does a task with token 1 have to be fulfilled in the first trick?
gold_answer: No. The token only means it must be fulfilled first among all the pending tasks, not in the first trick.

question: Two of my ordered tasks were won in the same trick. Did we fail?
gold_answer: Not if the task tokens are consecutive (e.g. 1 and 2): both count as correctly fulfilled regardless of which card was played first in that trick.

question: What is the difference between number tokens and arrow tokens?
gold_answer: Both specify an order, but arrow tokens are more flexible: they only constrain the relative order of the arrow-marked tasks among themselves, while other tasks can be fulfilled at any point. Number tokens fix the position of a task among all pending token-marked tasks.

question: How does communication work in a dead zone mission?
gold_answer: You still place a card meeting one of the three conditions (highest/only/lowest), but you may NOT place the radio token on it. Instead flip the token to red and put it next to the card — the crew must intuit which of the three conditions applies.

question: What does the disruption rule do?
gold_answer: Communication is completely blocked until the trick number shown; starting directly before that trick, normal communication rules apply again.

question: How does commander's decision work?
gold_answer: The specified number of task cards are placed FACE DOWN. After reviewing hands, the commander asks each crew member whether they feel fit to take on all tasks (answer only yes/no), then decides who receives them — the commander may not choose themselves. Task cards are only revealed after the crew member is chosen; that member must fulfill all the tasks.

question: In commander's distribution, can one player end up with many more tasks than another?
gold_answer: No. Tasks must be distributed evenly: at the end no one may have two tasks more than any other crew member. (The commander may choose themselves here, unlike commander's decision.)

question: We're playing with five players — what does the extra rule let us do?
gold_answer: From mission 25 on (missions marked with a golden frame around the mission number, plus missions 27 and 37), after cards and tasks are distributed but before the first trick, one crew member may hand over one of their task cards to another, decided jointly without revealing card information. It does not cost an extra attempt.

question: How does the two-player game work?
gold_answer: A third crew member, JARVIS, is simulated with a double row of 14 cards (7 face down, 7 face up on top; only face-up cards are playable, revealing the card beneath after the trick). The rocket 4 holder is commander, chooses JARVIS's tasks, and decides which of JARVIS's open cards to play, without discussion.

question: Is there a harder variant for three players?
gold_answer: Yes, 'Challenge for Three': return all green playing cards, green task cards, and the rocket 1 to the box, then play with normal rules. Only missions 13 and 44 are affected (you don't have to win a trick with the rocket 1).

question: In the logbook, what does the black pentagon with a number at the start of each mission mean?
gold_answer: It is the mission number. (A golden frame around it is a reminder of the additional five-player rule.)
notes: Requires reading the logbook's 'How to use this Logbook' legend; the pentagon itself is an unlabeled icon on every mission.

question: What does the golden frame around a mission number in the logbook mean?
gold_answer: It marks missions (25 and later) where the additional five-player rule applies: one crew member may hand a task card to another before the first trick.
notes: Answer spans both documents — logbook legend + rulebook five-player section.

question: What is the small circle printed next to each mission in the logbook for?
gold_answer: It is the distress signal field: circle it when the crew activates the distress signal token for that mission (which adds +1 to the recorded attempts). When activated, each crew member hands one of his or her cards to his or her neighbors. Rocket cards cannot be passed. The group decides together if the cards are passed to the left or right, but everyone must pass in the same direction. After cards are passed the mission starts with the standard rules. The distress signal stays active until the mission is completed, not matter how many tries are needed.

question: How can I tell how many task cards a mission uses?
gold_answer: The number is shown inside the red starburst task-card icon in the mission's entry in the logbook.
notes: Rules p8 shows the symbol with an example count; logbook p3 explains the icon.

question: What do the arrow symbols (single and double arrows) on task tokens mean?
gold_answer: Arrow tokens specify a relative order: a task with fewer arrows must be fulfilled before a task with more arrows, but unmarked tasks can be fulfilled at any time. They are more flexible than number tokens.
notes: VERIFY arrow semantics against the printed examples on p16.

question: In mission 9, what does the purple omega (Ω) symbol mean?
gold_answer: The omega task token means that task must be fulfilled last — with the final trick of the mission.
notes: VERIFY: whether omega means 'last of the marked tasks' or 'won in the very last trick', and the exact rulebook page where omega is defined — the token list on p14 extracts as icons with little text.

question: A mission shows a crossed-out radio/communication symbol. What rule applies?
gold_answer: That is the dead zone symbol: communication is limited. You place a communicated card as normal, but flip your radio token to red and put it NEXT to the card instead of on it — the crew must guess whether it's your highest, only, or lowest card.
notes: VERIFY which exact symbol (dead zone vs disruption) is the crossed-out radio; both are on p17.

question: A mission shows a communication symbol with a number in it. What does the number mean?
gold_answer: That is the disruption symbol: no communication is allowed until the numbered trick. Starting directly before that trick, normal communication resumes.
